summary.title: DU Hockey Ticket Management System
summary.description: Threat model for the DU Hockey Ticket Management System, a Flask-based web application with registration, login, ticket purchasing, refund requests, admin management, SQLite database storage, and session/token-based authentication.

data.name: User / Admin Authorization Boundary
data.description: Separates normal authenticated user functionality from privileged administrative functionality. Regular users should not be able to cross this boundary or perform admin-only actions.

data.name:
Registered
User
data.description: Authenticated regular user who can access user-only features such as browsing available games, reserving seats, purchasing tickets, viewing issued tickets/barcodes, and submitting refund requests for tickets they own.

data.name: Admin User
data.description: Authenticated administrator who can access the admin dashboard, manage users, suspend/reactivate accounts, elevate user roles, manage tickets, and approve or deny refund requests.

data.name:
Anonymous
Visitor
data.description: Unauthenticated visitor who can view public pages, register for an account, and attempt to log in.

data.name:
Browse
Games
data.description: Allows users to view available hockey games, sections, pricing, and seat availability before making a ticket reservation or purchase.

data.name:
Login/Register
+
Session Valiation
data.description: Handles account registration, login, password verification, CSRF validation, session/token creation, and authentication checks for protected routes.

threats.title: Session Token Forgery
threats.description: An attacker may forge, tamper with, or reuse a session cookie/token to impersonate another user if the application secret key is weak, exposed, or poorly protected. This could allow unauthorized access to user-only or admin-only features.
threats.mitigation: Use a cryptographically strong SECRET_KEY, protect secrets from source control and logs, enforce HTTPS, set Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite cookie flags, expire sessions appropriately, and validate sessions/tokens server-side on every protected request.

threats.title: Credential Stuffing Due to No Rate Limiting
threats.description: The login endpoint may allow repeated authentication attempts without rate limiting, lockout, CAPTCHA, or delay. An attacker could automate login attempts using breached email/password lists and eventually gain access to valid user accounts.
threats.mitigation: Add rate limiting to login attempts, temporarily lock accounts after repeated failures, add CAPTCHA or step-up verification after suspicious activity, and monitor logs for repeated failed login attempts from the same IP or account.

threats.title: Debug Mode Information Disclosure
threats.description: If debug mode is enabled in a non-development environment, unhandled errors may expose stack traces, source code snippets, local variables, file paths, configuration details, or interactive debugging features to users.
threats.mitigation: Disable debug mode outside development. Configure generic custom error pages for users, log detailed errors server-side only, and ensure sensitive configuration values are never displayed in error responses.

data.name:
Ticket
Purchase
data.description: Handles ticket reservation and purchase logic, including game selection, section selection, quantity validation, student discount checks, payment method submission, ticket creation, order creation, and barcode generation.

threats.title: Payment Method Field Manipulation
threats.description: A user may manipulate the payment method field in the browser using DevTools or a custom HTTP request and submit an arbitrary value that was not offered by the application UI. If the server accepts the value, fraudulent or invalid payment records could be stored.
threats.mitigation: Validate payment method values server-side using a strict allowlist. Reject any value outside the approved set with an error response. Consider database constraints and integration with a real payment processor instead of trusting client-submitted payment fields.

threats.title: Client-Side Quantity Manipulation
threats.description: A user may modify HTML min/max attributes, hidden fields, or request parameters to submit invalid ticket quantities such as zero, negative numbers, or excessive quantities. Relying only on browser-side restrictions would allow order manipulation.
threats.mitigation: Validate ticket quantity server-side on every purchase request. Enforce minimum and maximum quantity rules, check seat availability, reject invalid values, and avoid trusting client-side form restrictions.

data.name:
Refund
Request
data.description: Allows authenticated users to submit refund requests for tickets they own. The process verifies ticket ownership, prevents duplicate refund requests, and stores refund request status for admin review.

threats.title: Refund Queue Flooding
threats.description: A user or automated script may submit a high volume of refund requests, overwhelming the admin refund queue and making it difficult for administrators to process legitimate refund requests in a timely manner.
threats.mitigation: Rate limit refund requests per user and per IP address. Prevent duplicate refund requests for the same ticket, add cooldown periods, monitor abnormal refund volume, and alert admins when the queue grows unusually fast.

data.name:
Admin
Management
data.description: Handles privileged administrative actions such as viewing users, suspending or reactivating accounts, elevating users to admin, reviewing refund requests, approving or denying refunds, and managing ticket-related records.

threats.title: No Admin Action Audit Logging
threats.description: Sensitive admin actions such as suspending users, reactivating accounts, elevating users to admin, approving refunds, or denying refunds may not be recorded in an audit log. A malicious or compromised admin could perform actions and later deny responsibility.
threats.mitigation: Implement append-only audit logging for all sensitive admin actions. Each log entry should include actor user ID, target resource, action type, timestamp, IP address, request ID, and outcome. Restrict modification of audit logs.

threats.title: Uncontrolled Admin Elevation
threats.description: A compromised admin account may elevate additional users to admin without extra verification, approval, notification, or audit logging. This allows an attacker who compromises one admin account to expand and preserve privileged access.
threats.mitigation: Require MFA and re-authentication before role elevation. Add approval or notification for new admin creation, log all privilege changes, and apply least privilege by separating admin roles for user management, ticket management, and refund management.

data.name:
Application / Database
Trust Boundary
data.description: Separates the Flask application logic from the SQLite database. Data crossing this boundary should be validated, authorized, and safely queried or updated through trusted server-side logic.

data.name: Credentials + CSRF
data.description: The anonymous visitor submits registration or login data to the application. This may include username, email address, password, password confirmation, and CSRF token. This flow crosses the Internet/Application Trust Boundary and must be protected against credential theft, brute force attacks, CSRF, injection, and malformed input.

data.name: User lookup/write
data.description: The authentication process reads and writes user account records in the database. During registration, it creates a new user record and stores the password hash. During login, it looks up the submitted email or username, verifies the password hash, and checks account status and role information.

data.name: Secret key read
data.description: The authentication/session process reads sensitive configuration values such as SECRET_KEY or token-signing secrets to create, sign, or validate sessions/tokens. Exposure or weakness of these secrets could allow session forgery, impersonation, or privilege escalation.

data.name: Game/seat lookup
data.description: The application queries the database for available games, teams, sections, seat availability, and pricing information. These queries support the user-facing game browsing and ticket selection pages.

data.name: Session cookie
data.description: After successful authentication, the application returns an authenticated session cookie or token to the user’s browser. This value allows the user to access protected application features. It must be protected with secure session settings such as HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, expiration, and server-side validation.

data.name: Order details
data.description: The registered user submits ticket purchase or reservation details, including game ID, section ID, quantity, student ID if applicable, payment method, and CSRF token. This flow must be validated server-side because users can manipulate form fields, hidden values, dropdowns, and request parameters in the browser.

data.name: Seat/order write
data.description: The ticket purchase process checks seat availability, validates business rules, creates order records, creates ticket records, stores payment records, and updates seat or ticket status. This flow must prevent invalid quantities, payment method tampering, discount abuse, duplicate purchases, and unauthorized data modification.

data.name: Price/availability
data.description: The database returns pricing, section availability, seat information, order status, and ticket/barcode data to the ticket purchase process. The application uses this data to calculate totals, confirm purchases, and generate issued ticket information.

data.name: Refund request
data.description: The registered user submits a refund request for a ticket. The request may include ticket ID, refund reason, and CSRF token. The application must verify that the ticket belongs to the authenticated user before accepting the request.

data.name: Refund write/status
data.description: The refund request process checks ticket ownership, checks whether a refund request already exists, creates a refund request record, and updates refund status when necessary. This flow must prevent IDOR, duplicate refund requests, queue flooding, and unauthorized refund creation.

data.name: Ticket refund status
data.description: The database returns ticket ownership information, ticket status, and existing refund request status to the refund request process. The application uses this information to decide whether the current user is allowed to request a refund and whether the request is a duplicate.

data.name: Admin action
data.description: The admin user submits privileged administrative actions, such as suspending or reactivating users, elevating a user to admin, viewing users, managing tickets, and approving or denying refund requests. This flow crosses the User/Admin Authorization Boundary and must require strong server-side role enforcement.

data.name: User/tickets updates
data.description: The admin management process writes privileged changes to the database, including user status updates, role changes, ticket updates, refund approval or denial decisions, and other administrative modifications. This flow should be protected by authorization checks, CSRF protection, and audit logging.

data.name: Admin records
data.description: The database returns administrative records such as user lists, user roles, account statuses, ticket records, order records, payment records, and refund queue items. This data should only be available to authenticated administrators.

data.name: Admin results
data.description: The application returns admin dashboard views, user management results, ticket management results, and refund decision status messages to the admin user. The response may contain sensitive administrative information and should not be accessible to regular users.

data.name: Admin config read
data.description: The admin management process may rely on sensitive configuration values such as admin seed credentials, role configuration, SECRET_KEY, or environment-based settings. Exposure of these values could allow unauthorized privilege escalation or admin account compromise.

data.name: Confirmation + barcode
data.description: The application returns ticket purchase confirmation, order details, issued ticket information, and barcode/token information to the registered user. Because barcodes may function as ticket validity tokens, this response should avoid unnecessary exposure and should protect against information disclosure.

threats.title: Barcode Exposure in Ticket Listing
threats.description: Full ticket barcodes may be displayed in the user’s ticket listing or confirmation page. If barcodes function as ticket validity tokens, they could be exposed through screenshots, shoulder surfing, browser history, or shared pages.
threats.mitigation: Mask barcodes by default and reveal the full barcode only when necessary. Require re-authentication for sensitive ticket details, prevent unnecessary barcode exposure, and allow barcode rotation or invalidation if exposed.

data.name:
Internet/
Application Trust Boundary
data.description: Separates untrusted browser/client traffic from the Flask application. Requests crossing this boundary must be authenticated, authorized, validated, and protected against tampering.

data.name: SQLite Database
data.description: Persistent application database storing users, roles, password hashes, games, teams, sections, seats, section pricing, orders, tickets, payments, barcodes, and refund requests.

data.name:
Application Secrets/
Environment Variables
data.description: Runtime configuration and sensitive values used by the Flask application, including SECRET_KEY, admin seed credentials, database configuration, and other environment-specific settings.

data.name: Game browsing
data.description: An authenticated user requests available hockey games, sections, pricing, and seating information. This flow should only expose information appropriate for regular users and should not reveal internal database details, hidden admin information, or sensitive system state.

data.name: Game list
data.description: The application returns game listings, section options, seat availability, and pricing information to the authenticated user. The response should be properly encoded and should not expose unnecessary internal database fields or debugging information.

data.name: Refund status
data.description: The application returns refund request status to the registered user, such as pending, approved, denied, or already requested. The response should only reveal refund information for tickets owned by the authenticated user.
